findings.description: In `src/api/app.py` lines ~126-150, the middleware addition order results in `Trace -> CORS -> SecurityHeaders -> Audit -> Auth` as the outermost-to-innermost runtime stack (Starlette's `add_middleware` pushes to the top/outer layer). This violates the spec's strict outermost-first requirement: `Trace -> Audit -> Auth -> CORS`. To achieve the spec's order, CORS must be added first (innermost) and Trace added last (outermost).
findings.resolution: Reorder the `app.add_middleware` calls in `src/api/app.py` to match the reverse of the desired execution stack: add CORS, then Auth, then Audit, then Trace.

findings.description: In `src/api/routes/audit_routes.py` line 67, the limit query parameter is declared as `limit: int = Query(default=100, ge=1)`. This enforces a `ge=1` validation constraint, meaning requests with `?limit=0` or `?limit=-1` will return a 422 Validation Error. This violates the spec requirement that limit is 'server-clamped, never fails' validation. There is also a corresponding test coverage gap, as negative limit cases are not tested in `test_audit_routes.py`.
findings.resolution: Remove `ge=1` from the `Query()` definition for the `limit` parameter so it passes request parsing and relies entirely on the existing `min(max(limit, 1), 1000)` clamping logic in the handler.

findings.description: In `src/api/routes/audit_routes.py` line 96, the `total_count` field of `AuditQueryResponse` is blindly set to `len(entries)`. Because `entries` is already truncated by the `LIMIT` clause (max 1000), `total_count` will never exceed the page size. This violates the standard semantic purpose of a total count field in a paginated payload.
findings.resolution: Execute a separate `SELECT COUNT(*) ...` query using the same `where_sql` filters (without the `LIMIT`/`ORDER BY` clauses) and bind that scalar result to `total_count`.
